title: Demystifying the Transition: CBDT's Comprehensive FAQs on Income Tax Act, 2025
content:
# Demystifying the Transition: CBDT's Comprehensive FAQs on Income Tax Act, 2025

The air has been thick with anticipation and, dare I say, a touch of apprehension, ever since the **Income Tax Act, 2025 (ITA 2025)** officially replaced our six-decade-old companion, the Income Tax Act, 1961 (ITA 1961), effective April 1, 2026. For us tax professionals, navigating this monumental shift has been a top priority. Recognizing the numerous questions bubbling up regarding its practical implementation, the Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has, once again, stepped in with much-needed clarity. Their comprehensive Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs), issued dated July 6, 2026, provide invaluable guidance, acting as a crucial compass during this transitional journey.

It’s natural to wonder, "Kya hoga humare pending cases ka?" or "How will my current year's compliance be affected?" These FAQs are precisely what we needed to iron out the creases and ensure a smooth sail into the new regime. Let’s dive deep into the key takeaways and understand what these clarifications mean for us and our clients.

## The Paradigm Shift: Understanding the New Landscape

The Income Tax Act, 2025, marks a significant reform aimed at simplifying India's direct tax system and streamlining compliance. It fundamentally reshapes how assessments, appeals, and dispute resolutions will be approached. While the core principles remain largely intact, the restructuring and renumbering of sections from over 800 in the old Act to 536 in the new one necessitate a careful re-acquaintance with the statute.

### Say Goodbye to 'Previous Year' and 'Assessment Year'

Perhaps one of the most prominent structural changes under the ITA 2025 is the introduction of the unified **'Tax Year'** concept. The previously bifurcated 'Previous Year' and 'Assessment Year' framework, which often led to confusion, has been replaced by a single 'Tax Year'. This 'Tax Year' aligns directly with the financial year, starting April 1 and ending March 31.

Here’s a quick comparison to put things in perspective:

| Feature                   | Old Provision (ITA 1961)                       | New Provision (ITA 2025)                         |
| :------------------------ | :--------------------------------------------- | :----------------------------------------------- |
| **Concept for Taxation**  | Previous Year (income earned) + Assessment Year (income assessed) | Single 'Tax Year' (income earned and assessed in the same period) |
| **Applicability Date**    | For income earned up to FY 2025-26             | For income earned from FY 2026-27 onwards |
| **Purpose of Change**     | Historical, sometimes confusing                | Simplification and clarity in compliance |

The CBDT has clarified that any reference to a "tax year" starting on April 1, 2025, or earlier, should be understood as a reference to the corresponding 'previous year' under the old Act. This transitional provision ensures continuity and does not alter the tax treatment for those earlier years.

## Navigating Pending Matters: What Stays, What Changes?

This is where the FAQs bring immense relief. A significant concern among taxpayers and professionals was the fate of ongoing assessments, appeals, and other proceedings. The CBDT has made it abundantly clear: **the law applicable depends on the tax year to which the transaction relates, not merely the date of action.**

### Continuity of Proceedings Under the Old Law

The core principle laid down is that all proceedings relating to **tax years beginning before April 1, 2026**, will continue to be governed by the **Income Tax Act, 1961**, even after the new law has come into force. This provides much-needed certainty.

*   **Pending Assessments and Appeals:** Any assessment, reassessment, or appeal that was pending before an authority, Tribunal, or Court on March 31, 2026, will be disposed of as if the 2025 Act had not been enacted. The 1961 Act will continue to govern that specific litigation until its final resolution. This means, 'पुराने मामले पुराने कानून से ही चलेंगे.'
*   **Fresh Appeals for Old Years:** Even if a new appeal is filed after April 1, 2026, but the matter relates to an Assessment Year prior to Tax Year 2026-27 (i.e., FY 2025-26 or earlier), it will follow the procedures of the 1961 Act.
*   **Search and Requisition Cases:** For search actions initiated before April 1, 2026, all connected proceedings, including block assessments, will remain under the 1961 Act.
*   **Tax Recovery Proceedings and Prosecutions:** These will also continue under the old law if they relate to tax years before April 1, 2026.

### Clarifications on Certificates and Approvals

*   **Lower Deduction/No Deduction Certificates (LDCs/NDCs):** While proceedings for earlier years continue under the old law, applications for LDCs or NDCs filed from April 1, 2026, onwards will be entirely covered by the provisions of the new Act, i.e., the Income Tax Act, 2025. This is a crucial distinction to note.
*   **Existing Approvals:** Most ongoing tax proceedings, pending applications, and existing approvals under the ITA 1961 will continue without interruption.

## Compliance in the New Era: ITR, Forms, and Payments

The transition naturally raises questions about day-to-day compliance. The CBDT's FAQs address these practical aspects directly.

*   **No Two ITRs:** A major relief is the clarification that taxpayers will **not** have to file two income tax returns during the transition period. Income earned during FY 2025-26 will continue to be reported through the return for Assessment Year (AY) 2026-27 under the ITA 1961, even if the return is filed after the new Act has come into force. The first return under the ITA 2025 will relate to income earned in FY 2026-27 (Tax Year 2026-27) and will become due only in 2027.
*   **Tax Payments – Choose Wisely!** The Board has strongly advised taxpayers to pay close attention while making tax payments. Self-assessment tax for FY 2025-26 should be paid against AY 2026-27 (governed by ITA 1961), whereas advance tax for income earned during FY 2026-27 will be governed by ITA 2025 and paid for Tax Year 2026-27. Selecting the wrong year could lead to mismatched tax credits and compliance headaches. *Ispe thoda dhyan dena padega, warna adjustment mein time lag jayega.*
*   **New Forms, Same PAN:** The good news is that existing PAN and TAN numbers remain valid; there's no need to apply for new ones. However, some procedural forms have been replaced or merged. For instance, Forms 15G and 15H have been consolidated into a single **Form 121** under the new law, and Form 60 has been replaced by **Form 97**. For TDS on property transactions, Form 26QB under ITA 1961 has been replaced by the new **Form 141** under ITA 2025.

## Preserving Rights: Losses, Refunds, and MAT Credit

Taxpayers will be happy to know that benefits earned under the old law are not lost simply because the new Act has taken effect.

*   **Carry Forward of Losses and Unabsorbed Depreciation:** Business losses and capital losses determined under the 1961 Act can continue to be carried forward under the new law, subject to existing conditions. The transition does not interrupt or restart the carry-forward period. Similarly, unabsorbed depreciation will also continue to be available.
*   **Pending Refunds:** Refund claims for excess TDS deducted under the old law or any other pending refunds under the ITA 1961 will continue to be processed and are still valid.
*   **MAT/AMT Credits:** Unutilised Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) or Alternate Minimum Tax (AMT) credits will also continue to remain available for set-off under the Income-tax Act, 2025.

## Specific Transitional Scenarios Addressed

The FAQs also touch upon more nuanced situations:

*   **Capital Gains Account Scheme:** If capital gains were deposited in the Capital Gains Account Scheme under ITA 1961 before April 1, 2026, and not utilized within the prescribed period, the unutilised portion will be taxed in the year the time limit expires. While taxability arises under ITA 2025, the computation will follow the provisions of ITA 1961.
*   **Overseas Remittances:** If an overseas remittance is made on or after April 1, 2026, for a liability that accrued before that date, the form filing requirements will apply as per the tax law in force on the date of remittance (i.e., ITA 2025). However, the taxability of the underlying income will be governed by ITA 1961, being the law applicable in the year of accrual.
*   **Residential Status:** Residential status is determined separately for each tax year. If an individual's stay spans across FY 2025-26 and FY 2026-27, tax residency for previous year 2025-26 will be determined based on the stay under ITA 1961, and for Tax Year 2026-27 under ITA 2025 provisions.

## The Bedrock: Section 536 of ITA 2025

At the heart of these transitional provisions lies **Section 536 of the Income Tax Act, 2025**, aptly titled "Repeal and Savings". This crucial section acts as the statutory backbone, ensuring that the repeal of the ITA 1961 does not create a vacuum or lead to a complete disruption of existing rights, liabilities, and ongoing proceedings. It's essentially the bridge that connects the old regime with the new, making sure *ki koi bhi cheez hawa mein na reh jaye*.

## Final Thoughts: Prudence and Proactive Compliance

The CBDT's FAQs are a welcome initiative, providing clarity and guidance during this significant legislative overhaul. As tax professionals, our role becomes even more critical in helping clients understand these nuances. It's not just about knowing the new sections, but understanding their interplay with the repealed Act for prior periods.

The key takeaways are clear: maintain separate records for FY 2025-26 and FY 2026-27, ensure correct Assessment Year/Tax Year selection for payments, and familiarize yourselves with the updated forms. While the new Act aims for simplification and modernization, the transitional period demands a vigilant and informed approach. *Thodi research, thoda dhyan – aur hum yeh transition bhi smoothly manage kar lenge!*

## Read the Official Document

*   [Download the Official Notification/Circular here](https://www.incometax.gov.in/iec/foportal/latest-updates)

*Published on: 10 July 2026*
